nativeRange.habitat: Typically inhabits deep coastal slopes and sheltered muddy or sandy substrates, usually at depths of 37–110 m; occasionally found shallower near seagrass beds with silty rubble. Has established itself in the southeastern Mediterranean via the Suez Canal.

diet.notes: Has been reported to consume benthic invertebrates including small crustaceans, worms, and mollusks, as well as plant-based matter. In captivity, has been documented accepting frozen mysis shrimp, Spirulina-enriched brine shrimp, krill, chopped squid, clams, mussels, and marine algae preparations. Generally considered a slow and selective feeder; live brine shrimp or bloodworms have been used to initiate feeding in newly introduced specimens. Feeding small portions two to three times daily is generally advised; floating foods are best avoided to reduce the risk of air ingestion.

summary: A box-shaped saltwater fish with a distinctive humped carapace, generally considered advanced-level due to its selective feeding, deep-water origins, and ability to release a lethal toxin when stressed.
careNotes:
The Helmet Cowfish (Tetrosomus gibbosus) is generally considered an advanced to expert-level species. Its most significant husbandry challenge is its potential to secrete ostracitoxin (pahutoxin) from its mucous glands when severely stressed or dying — this substance has been known to prove fatal to all other livestock in the aquarium, including the cowfish itself. This risk has been reported to be substantially reduced in stable, low-stress environments, but it makes the species unsuitable for tanks where it is likely to be harassed or startled.

In the wild, this species tends to inhabit deeper coastal slopes and muddy substrates at depths of 37–110 m, which is somewhat unusual for a commonly traded aquarium fish. In captivity, it has been described as a slow, deliberate swimmer that does poorly in high-flow tanks. Easy startling should be avoided; gradual lighting transitions are generally advised. The species has been documented spitting water at the surface when hungry, a trait considered a reliable feeding cue by experienced keepers.

This is commonly considered a solitary species; keeping two specimens together is generally not advised, and housing with other boxfish or cowfish species has been known to result in aggression and stress. It is generally advised to keep only one specimen per tank and to avoid fast or aggressive feeding tankmates that may out-compete it for food.

Tank-size disagreement across sources: LiveAquaria recommends a minimum of 180 gallons (681 L), while ReefApp and a Sea Level Aquarium reference both cite 2000 L. Given the species' need for low flow, open swimming space, and the wide source spread (>2×), a minimum of 1000 L has been used as a cautious round-number estimate biased toward the larger recommendation. dataStatus set to needs_review to flag this for human review.

Care-level disagreement: LiveAquaria rates this species 'Expert Only'; Tropical Marine Centre rates it 'Intermediate'; Fishkeeper.co.uk describes it as 'moderate to advanced'. The averaged assessment of 'advanced' has been applied, with the understanding that the primary risk factor (toxin release) is more of a tank-management consideration than a daily husbandry challenge.

Reef-safe disagreement: LiveAquaria rates it 'with caution' (will nibble tubeworms); three other sources describe it as not reef safe, citing predation on small invertebrates and crustaceans. The majority assessment of 'no' has been applied.

Temperature range was averaged across LiveAquaria (72–78°F = 22.2–25.6°C), FishBase (22.2–28.2°C), and Fishkeeper.co.uk (23–27°C): averaged min ~22°C, averaged max ~27°C.

FishBase notes the flesh and organs of this species are classified as poisonous, separate from the skin-secreted toxin — this is relevant for any collector or handler.

RESOLVED 2026-06-03: Tank-size source spread (265 L–2000 L across 5 sources) reflects real disagreement on toxin-stress space need vs. the species’ modest ~20 cm typical adult size. 1000 L retained as the conservative midpoint between the two most credible specialist sources (LiveAquaria 681 L; ReefApp 2000 L). Smaller retailer figures (FreshMarine 265 L, Quality Marine 284 L, LiveRockNReef 340 L) appear to size for juveniles rather than adults and do not account for the boxfish pahutoxin-release risk that small/crowded tanks amplify. minLengthCm of 180 cm pairs naturally with this 1000 L footprint. Human reviewer accepted the midpoint over the 2000 L maximalist value on 2026-06-03.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been reported in the hobby literature. No established captive-rearing techniques are known for this species.

additional.name: Tropical Marine Centre (TMC)
additional.notes: Listed as designated secondary in manifest. Provided care level (intermediate), reef safe (no), diet notes, and scientific name confirmation. Lacked size, temperature, tank size, and pH data. Care level disagreement with LiveAquaria (intermediate vs expert) — averaged to advanced.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 30 cm TL, common 20 cm), temperature range (22.2–28.2°C), taxonomy (Ostraciidae / Tetraodontiformes), native range, and habitat. Temperature averaged across sources.

additional.name: Fishkeeper.co.uk
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (23–27°C), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (SG 1.020–1.025), adult size (30 cm), diet, and reef-safe status (not recommended for reef). Confirmed majority reef-safe assessment of 'no'.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size 30 cm, native range, salinity ~SG 1.026, not reef safe, minimum tank 2000 L. Tank-size disagreement with LiveAquaria (2000 L vs 681 L) flagged in careNotes; biased toward larger value per playbook.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding reported in the hobby literature. No established rearing techniques are known.
